request.question: The online virtual world that was the subject of the Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. copyright case launched on what date?

title: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc.
Horrible Histories World
Bragg v. Linden Lab
Stevan Lieberman
Capitol Records, LLC v Vimeo, LLC
Active Worlds
Ty Girlz
Second Life
Cityspace
Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc
sentences: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. was a copyright case in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California involving a DMCA takedown notice dispute between companies that produce virtual animals on Second Life.
Ozimals filed a DMCA takedown notice, claiming that Amaretto’s horse infringed on their bunnies.
Consequently, Amaretto filed for a temporary restraining order against Linden Research, the makers of Second Life.
This was granted and held in effect as the case proceeded.
Horrible Histories World is a virtual world which is inspired by the "Horrible Histories" book series by Terry Deary.
The premise involves the player travelling back in time and experiencing history.
The game includes 5 different virtual worlds: Putrid Pirates, Rotten Romans, Terrible Tudors, Measly Middles Ages, and Awful Egyptians.
It was put together by Scholastic Children's Books and Yomego Social Media Agency, and officially launched in August 2011.
The world was built on the Huzutech platform.
The publisher reported in November 2011 that: "We now have over 36,000 registered users to the Horrible Histories virtual world.
We are continually adding new content and hopefully this will improve.
The latest additions include a new game called Rat Runs, a joke machine and daily facts."
Other planned features included an online virtual good shop, a moderated chat feature, and events where subscribers would meet Terry Deary or a historical character.

The publishing and commercial director of Scholastic Children’s Books - Lisa Edwards - said: "We are very proud of the online Horrible Histories experience we have created and are delighted to make it available to every fan, in every corner of the globe.
In this way, it more closely reflects the Pottermore model".
Bragg v. Linden Research, Inc., 487 F. Supp.
2007), was a civil action (No. 06-4925) removed to the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania in October 2006.

Linden Lab, an online virtual world service provider of "Second Life" terminated the account of a user Marc Bragg when it discovered that he had found a way to acquire virtual land at a lower-than-market price.
The user brought this suit, which was ultimately settled before a final decision was reached.

However, the District Court did decide on two issues which may be important in future virtual world litigation: that the Second Life Terms of Service’s mandatory arbitration provision was unenforceable; and that interaction with a person in a virtual world can satisfy a state’s "minimum contacts" requirement for personal jurisdiction.
Stevan Lieberman (born July 22, 1965 in Brooklyn, New York City) is an American lawyer, regarded as a legal expert in intellectual property law and patent law.
He is a partner of the Washington, D.C.-based law firm, Greenberg & Lieberman, with Michael Greenberg, established in 1996.

As part of the firm, Lieberman has been involved in hundreds of UDRP or trademark infringement disputes, and he is considered a pioneer in the technology-law areas of virtual worlds, domain names and software, cited by CNN as "among the virtual world's earning elite."

Among the first lawyers in the world to begin generating a significant revenue and client base via online virtual worlds such as Second Life; he is the co-founder and CEO of two virtual reality websites.
"Capitol Records, LLC v. Vimeo, LLC" was a 2013 copyright infringement case out of the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York.
The decision resolved cross-motions for summary judgment filed by a video-sharing service (Vimeo) and a pair of record labels.

Vimeo sought a ruling that, as a matter of law, it was entitled to safe harbor protection under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) as to a series of copyrighted videos that were uploaded to its platform; the record labels sought the opposite ruling.
Active Worlds is an online virtual world, developed by ActiveWorlds Inc., a company based in Newburyport, Massachusetts, and launched on June 28, 1995.
Users assign themselves a name, log into the Active Worlds universe, and explore 3D virtual worlds and environments that others have built.
ActiveWorlds allows users to own worlds and universes, and develop custom 3D content.
The browser has web browsing capabilities, voice chat, and basic instant messaging.
Ty Girlz are girl dolls manufactured by Ty Inc. Similar to the Ty Beanie Babies the Ty Girlz are on a limited release pattern with dolls being introduced and older ones retired at various times.
Ty Girlz are plush toys that are connected to an online virtual world at TyGirlz.com.
Introduced to the global market on April 13, 2007, Ty Girlz one of only very few virtual worlds geared to girls when it launched.
The line was discontinued in 2013, and the virtual world shut down on June 7 of that same year.
Second Life is an online virtual world, developed and owned by the San Francisco-based firm Linden Lab and launched on June 23, 2003.
By 2013, "Second Life" had approximately 1 million regular users.

In many ways, "Second Life" is similar to massively multiplayer online role-playing games; however, Linden Lab is emphatic that their creation is not a game: "There is no manufactured conflict, no set objective".
CitySpace was an internet-based virtual world launched at SIGGRAPH 1993 by educator and project director Zane Vella.
CitySpace was one of the earliest online virtual 3D environments and first came to attention via mainstream news media in late 1993.

CitySpace was also the first user-generated virtual world, similar to virtual worlds like Second Life, and enabled participants to contribute 3D computer graphics and digital imagery to a collaborative real-time rendered 3D virtual world in which participants interacted with each other via avatars.
CitySpace was active from 1993-1996 and won the 1996 NII Award for Arts and Entertainment.
Arista Records, LLC v. LAUNCH Media, Inc., 578 F.3d 148 (2d Cir.

N.Y. 2009), is a legal case brought by Arista Records, LLC, Bad Boy Records, BMG Music, and Zomba Recording LLC (collectively, "BMG") alleging that the webcasting service provided by LAUNCH Media, Inc. ("Launch") willfully infringed BMG’s sound recording copyrights.
The lawsuit concerns the scope of the statutory term “interactive service” codified in 17 U.S.C. § 114, as amended by the Digital Millennium Copyright Act of 1998 ("DMCA").

If the webcasting service is an interactive service, Launch would be required to pay individual licensing fees to BMG’s sound recording copyright holders; otherwise, Launch only need to pay “a statutory licensing fee set by the Copyright Royalty Board.”

title: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc.
Second Life

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

gold_support_titles: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc.
response.hop1_query: The online virtual world that was the subject of the Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. copyright case launched on what date?
hop1_docs: Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. | Amaretto Ranch Breedables, LLC v. Ozimals, Inc. was a copyright case in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California involving a DMCA takedown notice dispute between companies that produce virtual animals on Second Life.  Ozimals filed a DMCA takedown notice, claiming that Amaretto’s horse infringed on their bunnies.  Consequently, Amaretto filed for a temporary restraining order against Linden Research, the makers of Second Life.  This was granted and held in effect as the case proceeded.

Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc | Arista Records, LLC v. LAUNCH Media, Inc., 578 F.3d 148 (2d Cir.  N.Y. 2009), is a legal case brought by Arista Records, LLC, Bad Boy Records, BMG Music, and Zomba Recording LLC (collectively, "BMG") alleging that the webcasting service provided by LAUNCH Media, Inc. ("Launch") willfully infringed BMG’s sound recording copyrights.  The lawsuit concerns the scope of the statutory term “interactive service” codified in 17 U.S.C. § 114, as amended by the Digital Millennium Copyright Act of 1998 ("DMCA").  If the webcasting service is an interactive service, Launch would be required to pay individual licensing fees to BMG’s sound recording copyright holders; otherwise, Launch only need to pay “a statutory licensing fee set by the Copyright Royalty Board.”

Capitol Records, Inc. v. MP3Tunes, LLC | Capitol Records, Inc. v. MP3tunes, LLC is a 2011 case from the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York concerning copyright infringement and the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA).  In the case, EMI Music Group and fourteen other record companies (including Capitol Records) claimed copyright infringement against MP3tunes, which provides online music storage lockers, and MP3tunes's founder, Michael Robertson.  In a decision that has ramifications for the future of online locker services, the court held that MP3tunes qualifies for safe harbor protection under the DMCA.  However, the court found MP3tunes to still be liable for contributory copyright infringement in this case due to its failure to remove infringing songs after receiving takedown notices.  The court also held that Robertson is liable for songs he personally copied from unauthorized websites.

There (virtual world) | There is a 3D online virtual world created by Will Harvey and Jeffrey Ventrella.  There Inc. was founded in the spring of 1998.  Closed beta began in July 2001, with various stages of beta following, and ending with an October 2003 launch date.  On March 9, 2010 - one week after the announcement of its closure on March 2, 2010 - There.com shut its doors to the public.

Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. | Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. was a 2013 case from the United States District Court for the Southern District of New York concerning copyright infringement of digital music.  In "ReDigi", record label Capitol Records claimed copyright infringement against ReDigi, a service that allows resale of digital music tracks originally purchased from the iTunes Store.  Capitol Records' motion for a preliminary injunction against ReDigi was denied, and oral arguments were given on October 5, 2012.

Active Worlds | Active Worlds is an online virtual world, developed by ActiveWorlds Inc., a company based in Newburyport, Massachusetts, and launched on June 28, 1995.  Users assign themselves a name, log into the Active Worlds universe, and explore 3D virtual worlds and environments that others have built.  ActiveWorlds allows users to own worlds and universes, and develop custom 3D content.  The browser has web browsing capabilities, voice chat, and basic instant messaging.

Righthaven LLC v. Democratic Underground LLC | Righthaven LLC.  v. Democratic Underground LLC was a copyright infringement case which determined that a contract giving a party right to sue on behalf of a copyright holder does not give the party legal standing to file such lawsuits.  This case is one of over 200 similar cases filed by Righthaven against media outlets using content from Stephens Media.  Judge Roger L. Hunt ruled that Righthaven lacked standing to file a copyright infringement suit and ordered Righthaven to show cause within two weeks why it should not be sanctioned for failure to disclose Stephens Media as an interested party.

Arista Records, LLC v. Launch Media, Inc

Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc.
Righthaven LLC v. Democratic Underground LLC
response.summary_1: Passage evidence: the case "involv[ed] ... virtual animals on Second Life" and notes "Amaretto filed for a temporary restraining order against Linden Research, the makers of Second Life." Missing fact / bridge: the launch date of Second Life (the online virtual world at issue) is not provided. Retrieval direction & anchors: retrieve the official launch date for "Second Life" (search for "Second Life launch date" or the Linden Lab / Second Life encyclopedia page), using the quoted anchors "virtual animals on Second Life" and "Linden Research, the makers of Second Life" as confirmation that the target entity is Second Life.
response.hop2_query: Second Life launch date Linden Lab
hop2_docs: Second Life | Second Life is an online virtual world, developed and owned by the San Francisco-based firm Linden Lab and launched on June 23, 2003.  By 2013, "Second Life" had approximately 1 million regular users.  In many ways, "Second Life" is similar to massively multiplayer online role-playing games; however, Linden Lab is emphatic that their creation is not a game: "There is no manufactured conflict, no set objective".

Economy of Second Life | The virtual world "Second Life" has its own economy and a virtual token referred to as Linden Dollars (L$).  In the SL economy, users (called "residents") buy from and sell to one another directly, using the "Linden", which is a closed-loop virtual token for use only within the Second Life platform.  Linden Dollars have no monetary value and are not redeemable for monetary value from Linden Lab.  A resident with a surplus of Linden Dollars earned via a Second Life business or experiential play can request to refund their Linden Dollar surplus to PayPal.  This economy is independent of the price of the game, which users pay to Linden Lab, not to each other.  Linden Lab reports that the "Second Life" economy generated US$3,596,674 in economic activity during the month of September 2005, and as of September 2006 "Second Life" was reported to have a GDP of $64 Million.
Linden Lab | Linden Research, Inc., d/b/a Linden Lab, is a privately held American Internet company that is best known as the creator of Second Life.
Linden Scripting Language | Linden Scripting Language, or LSL, is the programming language used by residents of Second Life (SL), a virtual world by Linden Lab and similar virtual worlds.

Cory Ondrejka | Cory Ondrejka was vice president of engineering at Facebook.  He also was the Chief Technology Officer of Linden Lab, makers of Second Life.  With Philip Rosedale, Ondrejka co-founded Second Life and played a significant role in the architecture of the product.  After leaving Linden Lab, Ondrejka became Senior Vice President of Digital Strategy for the EMI Group until he left EMI in September 2009.
Real estate (Second Life) | Virtual real estate on "Second Life", an online world owned and operated by Linden Lab since 2003,

Criticism of Second Life | Due to constant development, and as an open environment accessible by almost anyone with access to the Internet, a number of difficulties have arisen around "Second Life".  Problems range from the technical (budgeting of server resources) to moral (pornography), legal (legal position of the Linden Dollar, "Bragg v. Linden Lab") and, in September 2006, security (customer security).
response.summary_2: The online virtual world at issue was Second Life, which launched on June 23, 2003.
